mnemonics: SPANS: Set of Possible Answers (to the question 'what can I reach with these vectors?')
keyPoints: The linear span of a set of vectors is the collection of all possible linear combinations of those vectors.
A set of vectors spans a vector space V if every vector in V can be written as a linear combination of the vectors in the set.
The span of any set of vectors is always a subspace of the vector space they belong to.
commonMistakes: Confusing a spanning set with a basis. A spanning set does not have to be linearly independent, while a basis must be.
Assuming that if {v1, v2} spans V, then any two vectors in V will also span V. This is not true; the new vectors must also be linearly independent and sufficiently numerous.
Thinking that adding more vectors to a spanning set will break the span. Adding vectors to a spanning set still results in a spanning set (though it may become linearly dependent).
